text.data-narration: Process Scheduling is the discipline of allocating a set of processes to various time slices, known as time quanta or time slices, within a given time period. The goal of process scheduling is to optimize the performance of the system by allocating sufficient CPU time to each process, minimizing context switching overhead, and maximizing overall system throughput.

There are two primary types of process scheduling algorithms: Shortest Job First (SJF) and Round Robin (RR). SJF schedules the process with the shortest execution time first. The RR algorithm schedules each process for a fixed time slice (time quantum) before moving on to the next process.

Preemption, context switching, CPU burst, turnaround time, and waiting time are some key characteristics of process scheduling algorithms. Preemption is the ability of a process to be interrupted and another process scheduled at any time. Context switching is the process of switching from one process to another. CPU burst is the actual time spent by a process executing on the CPU. Turnaround time is the total time a process spends from its arrival to its completion. Waiting time is the time a process spends waiting in the system before it is scheduled.

SJF has several advantages, including simplicity and low overhead. However, it can be prone to starvation and has a high waiting time. RR has several advantages, including fairness and low waiting time. However, it can be complex and have a high overhead.
